items.q: Does a falling birth rate immediately stop population growth?
items.correct: No — population momentum means a youthful age structure keeps growth going for decades, as large cohorts reach childbearing age even at lower fertility.
items.misconception: Once fertility falls to replacement level, the population stops growing straight away.
items.random_wrong: Populations only grow in leap years.
items.why: Momentum explains why projections keep rising after fertility declines — a frequent exam stumbling block.

items.q: Was Malthus right that population always outruns food supply?
items.correct: His prediction has not held so far — technology, the Green Revolution and trade raised food output faster than he foresaw, as Boserup argued, though resource limits remain debated.
items.misconception: Malthusian famine checks have operated exactly as predicted.
items.why: The Malthus-Boserup debate requires weighing both views, not declaring one a proven law.

items.q: Is overpopulation simply about high population density?
items.correct: No — overpopulation is when population exceeds carrying capacity given current resources and technology; a dense, wealthy area can be well supported while a sparse, arid one is overpopulated.
items.misconception: Crowded places are by definition overpopulated.
items.random_wrong: Overpopulation is measured by counting cars.
items.why: Linking population to resources rather than space is the core of the carrying capacity concept.

items.q: What is the replacement fertility rate?
items.why: It's 2.1. “1.5” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Boserup. “Malthus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: World average ecological footprint (gha per capita)?
items.why: The real figure is 3 gha. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
